section.title: Ehud and Shamgar
section.sectionRationale: The clean pattern of Othniel now turns again, and this time the narrator tells it with vivid, earthy detail and biting satire. Israel does evil once more, is handed to Eglon king of Moab, and cries out; God raises up an unlikely deliverer, Ehud, a left-handed Benjamite, who kills the fat king with a hidden blade and then routs Moab. A single verse then adds Shamgar, who strikes down six hundred Philistines with a farm tool. Together they show God saving through surprising, weak, and unexpected instruments.
section.thesis: God delivers His people not through the obvious or the impressive but through the overlooked and the improbable, a left-handed man from the tribe of the right hand and a farmer with an oxgoad, so that the deliverance is unmistakably His and the proud oppressor is brought low.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the servitude under Eglon of Moab, verses 12 to 14; the raising up and daring plan of Ehud, verses 15 to 23; the rout of Moab and the land's rest, verses 24 to 30; and the brief note of Shamgar, verse 31.

verses.text: Once again the Israelites did evil in the sight of the LORD. So He gave Eglon king of Moab power over Israel, because they had done evil in the sight of the LORD.
After enlisting the Ammonites and Amalekites to join forces with him, Eglon attacked and defeated Israel, taking possession of the City of Palms.
The Israelites served Eglon king of Moab eighteen years.

units.label: Ehud raised up with a hidden sword

verses.text: And again they cried out to the LORD, and He raised up Ehud son of Gera, a {{itter|left-handed}} Benjamite, as their {{moshia|deliverer}}. So they sent him with tribute to Eglon king of Moab.
Now Ehud had made for himself a double-edged sword a cubit long. He strapped it to his right thigh under his cloak
and brought the tribute to Eglon king of Moab, who was an obese man.
After Ehud had finished presenting the tribute, he ushered out those who had carried it.
But upon reaching the idols near Gilgal, he himself turned back and said, “I have a secret message for you, O king.” “Silence,” said the king, and all his attendants left him.
Then Ehud approached him while he was sitting alone in the coolness of his upper room. “I have a word from God for you,” Ehud said, and the king rose from his seat.
And Ehud reached with his left hand, pulled the sword from his right thigh, and plunged it into Eglon’s belly.
Even the handle sank in after the blade, and Eglon’s fat closed in over it, so that Ehud did not withdraw the sword from his belly. And Eglon’s bowels emptied.
Then Ehud went out through the porch, closing and locking the doors of the upper room behind him.

units.label: The rout of Moab and the land's rest
units.note: the LORD has delivered your enemies

verses.text: After Ehud was gone, Eglon’s servants came in and found the doors of the upper room locked. “He must be relieving himself in the cool room,” they said.

So they waited until they became worried and saw that he had still not opened the doors of the upper room. Then they took the key and opened the doors, and there was their lord lying dead on the floor.
Ehud, however, had escaped while the servants waited. He passed by the idols and escaped to Seirah.
On arriving in Seirah, he blew the ram’s horn throughout the hill country of Ephraim. The Israelites came down with him from the hills, and he became their leader.

“Follow me,” he told them, “for the LORD has delivered your enemies the Moabites into your {{yad|hand}}.” So they followed him down and seized the fords of the Jordan leading to Moab, and they did not allow anyone to cross over.
At that time they struck down about ten thousand Moabites, all robust and valiant men. Not one of them escaped.
So Moab was subdued under the hand of Israel that day, and the land had rest for eighty years.
After Ehud came Shamgar son of Anath. And he too {{yasha|saved}} Israel, striking down six hundred Philistines with an oxgoad.

itter.gloss: The phrase for Ehud's left-handedness means literally bound or restricted in his right hand. The irony is sharp: Ehud is a Benjamite, and Benjamin means son of the right hand, yet this son of the right hand is a left-handed man. What looks like a limitation is exactly what God uses, for a blade strapped to the right thigh would never be searched for on a left-handed man.

moshia.gloss: A deliverer or saviour, from the verb to save, yasha. As with Othniel, it is the LORD who raises up the moshia; the human deliverer, however unlikely, is God's chosen instrument. The same word describes the LORD Himself as Saviour, Isaiah 43:11, and points forward to the ultimate Deliverer, Jesus.

yad.gloss: Hand, power, agency. The word threads through the chapter: Israel is given into the hand of Eglon, and then the LORD delivers the enemy into Israel's hand, and Moab is subdued under the hand of Israel. The reversal of the hand marks who holds the power, and the passage makes plain that the deciding hand is the LORD's.

yasha.gloss: To save, deliver, rescue. The signature verb of the book: even Shamgar, given only one verse and armed with a mere farm tool, saved Israel. The word insists that rescue comes from God through whatever instrument He chooses, and it is the root behind the name Jesus, the LORD saves, Matthew 1:21.

basic.context: The cycle turns a second time. Israel does evil again, so the LORD strengthens Eglon king of Moab, who with Ammonite and Amalekite allies seizes the City of Palms, Jericho, and rules Israel eighteen years. When Israel cries out, God raises up Ehud, a left-handed man from Benjamin, the tribe whose name means son of the right hand. Ehud forges a short double-edged sword, straps it to his right thigh where no one would search a left-handed man, and gains a private audience with the enormously fat king under the pretense of a secret message. He drives the blade in, escapes, rallies Israel, and they cut down Moab at the Jordan fords, winning eighty years of rest. A single closing verse adds Shamgar, who strikes down six hundred Philistines with an oxgoad, a simple farm tool. The question the passage presses is: through whom does God save? And the answer is startling, through the unlikely, the overlooked, and the weak.
basic.hermeneutics: This is vivid historical narrative laced with satire against Moab and its bloated king. It must be read as description, not prescription: the text reports Ehud's assassination of a foreign oppressor as an act of God-sent deliverance in a specific redemptive-historical moment; it does not hand believers a mandate for political violence today. The narrator's earthy, even comic detail, the hidden blade, the fat swallowing the handle, the servants assuming the king is merely relieving himself, mocks the proud oppressor and magnifies the God who topples him through the least likely means.
basic.originalLanguages: Ehud is itter yad-yemino, literally bound in his right hand, that is left-handed, and the irony bites because he is a Benjamite, a son of the right hand. The LORD raises him up as a moshia, a deliverer. The word yad, hand, runs through the story: Israel is in Eglon's hand until the LORD gives the enemy into Israel's hand. And Shamgar, in a single verse, yasha, saved, Israel, the same root that stands behind the name Jesus.
basic.typology: These unlikely deliverers foreshadow the way God saves in Christ. God chooses the weak and the overlooked to shame the strong, and supremely He saves through a crucified Messiah, which looked to the world like weakness and folly. Ehud striking the enemy a decisive, hidden blow and Shamgar's improbable victory both anticipate the greater, stranger victory of the cross.
basic.christ: God delivers His people through an unexpected saviour who strikes the oppressor a mortal blow, then through a lone man with a farm implement. This pattern reaches its climax in Jesus, who saves not with obvious power but through apparent weakness, a carpenter's son, a crucified King, striking the true enemy a death blow at the cross, where God chose the foolish and weak things of the world to shame the strong and to save.
basic.god: God is just: He hands His idolatrous people over to Moab. He is merciful: He hears their cry and raises up a rescuer. And He is gloriously free in His choice of instruments: a left-handed assassin and a farmer with an oxgoad. By saving through such means He makes it unmistakable that the deliverance is His own, so that no one can boast in human strength.
basic.crossReferences: Numbers 22 to 24 recall Moab's earlier hostility to Israel through Balak and Balaam. Judges 20 notes the left-handed slingers of Benjamin, a tribal trait God turns to account. First Corinthians 1 explains God's pattern of saving through the weak and foolish things to shame the strong. And Matthew 1 names Jesus for the salvation, yasha, that all these deliverers foreshadow.
basic.oneStory: Israel sins again and is enslaved again, and again God raises up an improbable saviour to win them rest. The satire on mighty Moab and its fat king, undone by a hidden blade, and the farmer who routs the Philistines with an oxgoad, all proclaim that salvation is God's doing through instruments the world would never pick. It is one more turn toward the great deliverance, when God would save the world through a crucified Messiah, the ultimate unexpected Deliverer.

context.literary: This is the second turn of the cycle, and where Othniel's account was spare, Ehud's is richly told and pointedly satirical. The narrative lingers on physical detail, the concealed cubit-long blade, the king's obesity, the fat closing over the hilt, the emptied bowels, the servants waiting outside the locked doors, to mock the proud oppressor and heighten the surprise of God's method. The single verse on Shamgar functions as a hinge and a coda, extending the theme of unlikely deliverance before the story of Deborah opens.
context.historical: Moab lay east of the Dead Sea and had a long history of hostility to Israel. The City of Palms is Jericho, a strategic foothold west of the Jordan. Tribute, the payment Ehud brings, was the tax a subject people paid an overlord, and it gave him lawful access to the king. The oxgoad Shamgar wields was a long pointed stick used to drive oxen, an ordinary farm tool rather than a weapon, underscoring how God saves through the unimpressive.
context.canonical: Ehud and Shamgar continue the downward cycle of Judges while displaying God's persistent mercy. The theme of God choosing the weak and unexpected instrument runs from these judges through Gideon's tiny band and David's sling to its climax in the crucified Christ, 1 Corinthians 1:27-29. The repeated giving into the hand and delivering out of the hand echoes the covenant blessings and curses and the Bible's larger story of bondage and rescue that finds its end in the greater exodus won by Jesus.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show, with vivid and satirical narrative, that God saves His people through surprising and improbable instruments, humbling a proud oppressor and making plain that the deliverance is His, so that the reader marvels at the God who topples the mighty through the overlooked.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes Ehud's assassination of Eglon and Shamgar's slaughter of Philistines as God-sent acts of deliverance in a particular moment of redemptive history; it does not prescribe assassination or vigilante violence for believers today. What endures and is meant to be imitated is the crying out to God, the trust that the LORD gives the enemy into His people's hand, and the confession that salvation comes from God through means of His own choosing.

debates.question: How should we read Ehud's deception and killing of Eglon, morally?

views.label: A God-sent deliverance to be received, not copied
views.case: The narrator frames Ehud as a deliverer whom the LORD raised up, and the killing as the means by which God freed His oppressed people in a specific covenantal moment of holy war; it is reported approvingly within that redemptive-historical setting, not offered as a timeless ethic of political murder.

views.label: A morally tangled act the text does not sanitize
views.case: Scripture records the cunning, the concealed blade, and the gruesome detail without moralizing, and honesty requires us to feel the tension; the passage shows God working through flawed and violent human means without endorsing every feature of them, much as it does elsewhere in Judges.

debates.bearing: Either way the deliverance is God's and the violence belongs to the world of holy war under the old covenant; Christians read it as description that magnifies God's saving power, not as license, and they long instead for the King who overcomes His enemies by dying for them.

debates.question: Why does the text dwell on Ehud's left-handedness and Eglon's obesity?

views.label: Deliberate irony and satire
views.case: The Benjamite, son of the right hand, is left-handed, and the fat king is undone by the very blade his guards would never find; the details are pointed irony that mocks proud Moab and delights in God's surprising method of rescue.

views.label: Realistic detail that explains the victory
views.case: The left hand explains why the sword on the right thigh escaped detection, and the king's size explains why the blade was swallowed whole; the details are not merely comic but the concrete means by which the plan succeeded under God's providence.

debates.bearing: Both readings serve the same point: what looked like Ehud's limitation was exactly what God used, teaching that God's deliverance often comes through what the world overlooks or despises.

study.originalLanguages: The story is built on a few telling words. Ehud is described as itter yad-yemino, literally bound or restricted in his right hand, the Hebrew idiom for left-handed, and the phrase drips with irony because Ehud belongs to Benjamin, a name that means son of the right hand; the son of the right hand is a left-handed man, and that very trait is what God uses, since no one would search for a blade on the right thigh of a left-handed man. The LORD raises him up as a moshia, a deliverer or saviour, the same word used of Othniel and of the LORD Himself. Running through the whole chapter is the word yad, hand, which means hand, power, and agency: Israel is given into the hand of Eglon, then the LORD gives the enemy into Israel's hand, and Moab is finally subdued under the hand of Israel. The shifting of the hand tells you who holds the power, and the answer is the LORD. Finally, of Shamgar, armed with nothing but an oxgoad, the text simply says he yasha, saved, Israel, the signature verb of the book and the root behind the name Jesus, the LORD saves. The vocabulary itself preaches that God rescues through instruments the world would never choose.

typology.type: The unlikely, overlooked deliverer who strikes the oppressor a decisive blow
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who appeared in weakness and lowliness and struck the true enemy a mortal blow at the cross, 1 Corinthians 1:27-29 and Hebrews 2:14.

typology.type: The single lowly instrument, an oxgoad, winning a great victory
typology.fulfillment: God saving the world through the folly and weakness of the cross, so that no flesh may boast, 1 Corinthians 1:25-31.

typology.type: The enemy given into the hand of God's people
typology.fulfillment: Christ handing His people victory over sin, death, and the devil, and giving them to share in His triumph, Colossians 2:15 and Romans 16:20.

study.christ: The way God saves here, through a left-handed man the enemy underestimated and a farmer with a cattle prod, is the very pattern that reaches its climax in Jesus. God delights to choose the weak and the overlooked to shame the strong, and He did so supremely in a crucified Messiah, 1 Corinthians 1:27-29. Ehud struck the oppressor a hidden, decisive blow and then led his people out of bondage; Christ struck the true oppressor, the devil who held the power of death, a mortal blow at the cross, and leads His people out of a far deeper slavery, Hebrews 2:14-15. Shamgar's improbable victory with a mere oxgoad shows the same principle that the cross displays in full: God saves by means the world calls foolish, so that salvation is unmistakably His and no one can boast. Where these deliverers won a rest that later lapsed, Jesus, the true and final Deliverer, wins a rest that never ends.
study.god: God fills this account as its true actor. He is just, giving His idolatrous people over to Eglon of Moab exactly as their sin deserved, and letting the servitude run eighteen long years. Yet He is merciful, hearing their cry and raising up a rescuer without delay. And He is sovereignly free in the instruments He chooses: not a mighty warrior of obvious strength but a left-handed man and, in a single verse, a farmer with an oxgoad. By stooping to save through such unlikely means, God makes the deliverance transparently His own work, mocking the pride of the strong and refusing to let human power take the credit. The reversal of the hand, from Eglon's grip to Israel's, shows that the deciding power was never Moab's and never merely Ehud's, but the LORD's, who gives the enemy into His people's hand.

crossReferences.note: Moab's earlier hostility to Israel under Balak, hiring Balaam to curse them, sets the backdrop for Moab as oppressor here.
Benjamin was known for left-handed slingers who could sling a stone to a hair and not miss; God turns a tribal trait to His saving purpose in Ehud.
God chose the weak and foolish things of the world to shame the strong, so that no one may boast, the pattern seen in Ehud and Shamgar and fulfilled at the cross.
Christ shared in flesh and blood so that through death He might destroy the one who held the power of death and free those in slavery, the greater deliverance these judges foreshadow.
You shall call His name Jesus, for He will save His people from their sins; the same root yasha that describes Shamgar's saving of Israel.

study.oneStory: Israel sins again and is sold into servitude again, and again God raises up an improbable saviour to win them rest, this time with pointed satire on mighty Moab and its bloated king, undone by a hidden blade, and a lone farmer routing the Philistines with an oxgoad. The whole account proclaims that salvation belongs to the LORD, who saves through instruments the world would never pick. It is one more turn in the downward cycle of the judges and one more rehearsal of the great deliverance to come, when God would save the world not through obvious might but through a crucified Messiah, Jesus, the ultimate unexpected Deliverer, in whom the world's weakness proves to be the power of God.

quiz.q: Why did Israel come under the power of Eglon king of Moab?

options.text: Because the Israelites again did evil in the sight of the LORD.
options.note: Verse 12 states plainly that God gave Eglon power over Israel because they did evil.

options.text: Because Moab had formed a strong alliance with Ammon and Amalek.
options.note: Eglon did enlist those allies, verse 13, but the stated reason for the servitude is Israel's sin.

options.text: Because Israel had no weapons to defend itself.
options.note: The text does not blame a lack of weapons; it names Israel's evil as the cause.

options.text: Because the LORD was powerless against Moab's gods.
options.note: The LORD Himself gave Eglon the power and later took it away; He is sovereign throughout.

quiz.q: How did Ehud's left-handedness matter to the deliverance?

options.text: He hid the sword on his right thigh, where guards would not search a left-handed man, and struck by surprise.
options.note: Verses 16 and 21 show the concealed blade drawn with the left hand; the trait was key to the plan.

options.text: It made him a more skilled and dangerous fighter overall.
options.note: Benjamites were noted for left-handed skill, but the point here is the concealed, unsearched blade.

options.text: It was an incidental detail with no bearing on the story.
options.note: Far from incidental, it is the very feature God used to accomplish the rescue.

options.text: It meant he needed no weapon at all to defeat the king.
options.note: He forged and used a double-edged sword; the left hand is how he concealed and wielded it.

quiz.q: What does Shamgar's victory with an oxgoad chiefly teach?

options.text: God saves through unlikely, lowly instruments, so the deliverance is clearly His.
options.note: A farm tool striking down six hundred shows salvation comes from God, not impressive means.

options.text: That a determined person can overcome great odds.
options.note: There is boldness here, but the deeper point is that God saved through him, verse 31.

options.text: That farmers made the best soldiers in Israel.
options.note: This misses the theology; the oxgoad highlights God's power, not a class of soldier.

options.text: That skilled weaponry is the key to victory.
options.note: The opposite: an ordinary oxgoad, not fine weaponry, wins the day under God's hand.

quiz.q: How should we read the killing of Eglon by Ehud?

options.text: As a God-sent deliverance reported within the old covenant, described for us, not prescribed as a pattern to copy.
options.note: The narrator frames Ehud as a raised-up deliverer; it is description in a redemptive-historical setting, not a license.

options.text: As a rough act God used, which the text records honestly without sanitizing.
options.note: True to the earthy honesty of the text, but it understates that God Himself raised Ehud up to deliver.

options.text: As a timeless example that believers may take up violence for God's causes.
options.note: This wrongly turns description into prescription; the New Testament calls Christ's people to another way.

options.text: As proof that the Bible approves of murder in general.
options.note: The passage is a specific act of deliverance under holy war, not any blanket approval of murder.

quiz.q: How do Ehud and Shamgar point forward to Christ?

options.text: God saves through the weak and unexpected, foreshadowing salvation through the crucified Messiah who strikes the true enemy a mortal blow.
options.note: The pattern of God using the overlooked climaxes in the cross, 1 Corinthians 1:27-29 and Hebrews 2:14.

options.text: They show that God's deliverers are always mighty and impressive figures.
options.note: The reverse is true; their unlikeliness is the very link to Christ's saving through weakness.

options.text: They teach that deliverance is only political and military.
options.note: Their earthly rescue points beyond itself to Christ's rescue from sin, death, and the devil.

options.text: They have no bearing on Christ or the gospel.
options.note: The root yasha, to save, that describes Shamgar stands behind the very name of Jesus.

questions: God raised up a left-handed man from the tribe of the right hand, using the very thing others might have counted a weakness. Where might God be wanting to use what you consider your limitation for His purposes?
The narrative openly mocks proud, bloated Moab, undone in a moment. Where do you see the pride of the strong today, and how does it steady you to know that God topples it in His time?
Israel's rescue came only after they cried out to the LORD. What burden do you need to bring to God in an honest cry rather than trying to carry it alone?
Shamgar saved Israel with nothing more than a farm tool. What ordinary, unimpressive means has God placed in your hand that He might use for good if you offer it to Him?

These deliverers won a rest that eventually lapsed, but Christ saves through the weakness of the cross and wins a rest that never ends. How does the strange power of the cross reshape what you count as strength and weakness this week?

translationNotes.note: The City of Palms is Jericho, as the BSB footnote notes; it was a strategic stronghold west of the Jordan that Moab seized.
Left-handed renders a Hebrew idiom meaning restricted in his right hand; the irony is that Ehud is a Benjamite, and Benjamin means son of the right hand.
A cubit long is about eighteen inches or forty-six centimeters, per the BSB footnote; a short blade well suited to concealment under a cloak.
An oxgoad was a long pointed staff used to drive oxen, an ordinary farm implement, which underscores how God saved Israel through the humblest of means.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Judges 3:12-31. The em dash in the source at Judges 3:25 has been converted to a comma. No em or en dashes present.
